summary:
The Sannai-Maruyama site in Aomori Prefecture, northern Honshu, Japan, is one of the most important Jōmon period settlements discovered in Japan, with its main occupation phase dating from approximately 3900 to 2300 BCE — well after the period covered here. However, the earliest proto-phase activity at the site and the broader Jōmon culture it represents have roots extending into the period from around 6000 BCE, when the Jōmon cultural tradition was already well established across the Japanese archipelago.

The Jōmon culture, of which Sannai-Maruyama is a later monument, is remarkable for producing some of the world's earliest pottery while remaining fundamentally based on a hunting, fishing, and gathering economy. Unlike the pottery-using farming cultures of Eurasia that arose in association with agriculture, Jōmon pottery appears as early as 14,000 to 13,000 BCE among sophisticated forager communities in Japan — a fact that has significantly complicated global models of pottery origins and the relationship between ceramics and food production.

By 6000 BCE, Jōmon communities were distributed widely across the Japanese archipelago, exploiting a diverse range of marine and terrestrial resources with great sophistication. Their seasonal settlements, elaborate ritual practices, and complex material culture — including decorated cord-marked pottery, clay figurines, and lacquerware — demonstrate that high cultural complexity and social organization need not depend on food production. Sannai-Maruyama's proto-phase represents the long roots of this remarkable forager tradition.
